title: Build and task commands

publication.changeSummary: Topic-specific draft for Build and task commands; requires human source, technical, pedagogy, and accessibility review. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

canonicalExplanation.summary: A build transforms declared source and assets into validated artifacts through repeatable commands whose inputs, outputs, environment, and failure status are explicit.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: A clean build in a fresh workspace produces the same tested bundle from the lockfile rather than relying on a developer's old output directory. Your goal is to run a clean checkout through setup, build, test and start without undocumented manual steps. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Ignore policy; Commits and history; Dependency manifests and lockfiles; pf tools terminal editor. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Operational model
sections.body: A build transforms declared source and assets into validated artifacts through repeatable commands whose inputs, outputs, environment, and failure status are explicit. The learner should use this model to predict an observable result rather than merely repeat terminology.

sections.heading: Boundary and failure point
sections.body: Caches, generated code, timestamps, native tools, network access, environment variables, and undeclared local files can make builds non-reproducible. A correct explanation therefore includes the applicable assumptions and avoids this common mistake: If the application runs from the editor, the documented clean build and its artifact are guaranteed to work elsewhere.

includes: Expose documented, non-interactive build, test, lint and run commands through the language's normal tooling.
Run a clean checkout through setup, build, test and start without undocumented manual steps.
excludes: Caches, generated code, timestamps, native tools, network access, environment variables, and undeclared local files can make builds non-reproducible.

outcomes: Run a clean checkout through setup, build, test and start without undocumented manual steps.

examples.title: Build and task commands: direct case
examples.situation: A clean build in a fresh workspace produces the same tested bundle from the lockfile rather than relying on a developer's old output directory.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Observe the starting evidence: A clean build in a fresh workspace produces the same tested bundle from the lockfile rather than relying on a developer's old output directory.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Check the result against this correction: Run the declared clean build with pinned dependencies, inspect exit status and artifact, and remove reliance on undeclared machine state.
examples.result: The case supports the declared outcome because Run the declared clean build with pinned dependencies, inspect exit status and artifact, and remove reliance on undeclared machine state.

examples.title: Build and task commands: boundary case
examples.situation: A failing compile exits nonzero and stops packaging instead of emitting a seemingly successful partial artifact.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Identify the changed context: A failing compile exits nonzero and stops packaging instead of emitting a seemingly successful partial artifact.
Step 2 — Keep this boundary explicit: Caches, generated code, timestamps, native tools, network access, environment variables, and undeclared local files can make builds non-reproducible.
Step 3 — Reject the documented misconception: If the application runs from the editor, the documented clean build and its artifact are guaranteed to work elsewhere.
examples.result: This case demonstrates the declared outcome: Run a clean checkout through setup, build, test and start without undocumented manual steps.

misconceptions.mistakenBelief: If the application runs from the editor, the documented clean build and its artifact are guaranteed to work elsewhere.
misconceptions.correction: Run the declared clean build with pinned dependencies, inspect exit status and artifact, and remove reliance on undeclared machine state.
misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: Using the direct and boundary scenarios, explain why this belief fails: If the application runs from the editor, the documented clean build and its artifact are guaranteed to work elsewhere.

analogy.example: A recipe and packing line can model Build and task commands: declared ingredients resolve to exact supplies, controlled steps produce an artifact, and labels preserve provenance.
usefulFor: Understanding dependency resolution, configuration, repeatable builds, and generated artifacts
limitations: Software supply chains include executable dependencies, caches, signatures, platform variance, and secret handling beyond a kitchen process.
Caches, generated code, timestamps, native tools, network access, environment variables, and undeclared local files can make builds non-reproducible.

artifact: A clean build in a fresh workspace produces the same tested bundle from the lockfile rather than relying on a developer's old output directory.
A build transforms declared source and assets into validated artifacts through repeatable commands whose inputs, outputs, environment, and failure status are explicit.

recap.summary: You should now be able to run a clean checkout through setup, build, test and start without undocumented manual steps. Keep this boundary in view: Caches, generated code, timestamps, native tools, network access, environment variables, and undeclared local files can make builds non-reproducible.
